Here’s a recipe you can follow on every continuous‐probability or percentile question, plus a short discussion of what happens when you round age to the nearest year.

1. Sketching a probability   
   – Draw the horizontal axis for X (age, weight, time, …).  
   – Sketch the density curve f (x) (or at least its general shape).  
   – To compute P(a < X < b), simply shade the area under f (x) from x=a to x=b.  
   – The numerical answer is ∫ₐᵇ f (x) dx.  

   Example (informal): if X is uniform on [0,100], P(20 ≤ X ≤ 30) is the area of the rectangle of base 10 and height 1/100, namely 10·(1/100)=0.10.  
 
2. Finding percentiles   
   – Draw the same density curve and (if you like) its corresponding cumulative F (x) rising from 0 to 1.  
   – If you want the p-th percentile xₚ, you solve F (xₚ)=p (i.e. you find the vertical line so that the area to its left is p).  
   – On your sketch, shade the area under f (x) from the far left to xₚ so that the shaded area equals p.  

   Example (formal): in the uniform[0,100] example, the 75th percentile x₀.₇₅ solves (x₀.₇₅–0)/(100–0)=0.75 ⇒ x₀.₷₅=75.  

3. Rounding age to the nearest year: continuous or discrete?  
   As long as you record exact (or sufficiently fine) ages, X can in principle take any nonnegative real value ⇒ it’s a continuous variable and you model it with a density.  
   However, the moment you “round to the nearest year,” you collapse each half‐year interval [k–0.5, k+0.5) onto the single label k.  As a result:  
   • Only integers …, 0,1,2,3,… can ever appear.  
   • P(“age”=k) is now positive (it’s P(X∈[k–0.5,k+0.5))), so you have point‐masses at each integer.  
   ⇒ The rounded data are discrete.  

In summary, continuous‐variable problems are always handled by shading areas under a smooth density curve (or using F (x) for percentiles).  But once you round to the nearest whole year your measurements become a discrete distribution on the integers.

2.  **Definition of Discrete Data:** A discrete variable can only take on specific, distinct, and countable values. There are gaps between the possible values. For example, the number of pets you own can be 2 or 3, but it cannot be 2.5. It is **counted**.

3.  **The Effect of Rounding:** When you round age to the nearest year, you are forcing the data into a limited set of values. You can no longer have a value between 30 and 31. The infinite possibilities that exist in the continuous range have been collapsed into distinct, countable integers.
